dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use two pointers when the array is sorted and you need to find a pair, triplet, or subarray satisfying a condition. If you see 'find two numbers that sum to X' or 'remove duplicates in-place', two pointers is your first instinct.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Maintain a left and right pointer and move them toward each other based on some condition. This eliminates the need for a nested loop, bringing O(n²) down to O(n). The key insight is that sorting gives you directional information — if the sum is too large, move right pointer left; if too small, move left pointer right.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Students forget to handle duplicates when the problem asks for unique pairs or triplets — add a while loop to skip same elements after each valid pair. Also don't apply two pointers on unsorted arrays without first sorting.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Say out loud: 'Since the array is sorted, I can use two pointers to avoid the O(n²) brute force — this gives me O(n) after the sort.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use fixed sliding window when the problem asks for max/min/sum of a subarray of exactly k elements. Keywords: 'subarray of size k', 'every window of length k'.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Maintain a window of exactly k elements. Add the new element entering the window, subtract the element leaving it. You never recompute the whole window — just update the delta. This is always O(n) instead of O(n*k).
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Students often recompute the sum from scratch each step, which defeats the purpose. Also, the window starts when you've seen exactly k elements, so your first valid answer is at index k-1, not index 0.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Frame it as: 'I'm maintaining a running sum and just adjusting for what enters and exits — constant time per step.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use variable sliding window when you need the longest or shortest subarray satisfying a condition. Classic triggers: 'longest substring without repeating characters', 'smallest subarray with sum >= k'.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Expand the right pointer to grow the window, shrink the left pointer when the window becomes invalid. The key is defining clearly what 'valid' means and maintaining a data structure (hashmap, counter) to check validity in O(1). Track the max/min window size throughout.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: The most common bug is shrinking the window by only one step when you should keep shrinking until it's valid again. Use a while loop to shrink, not an if statement.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Describe your invariant upfront: 'At all times, the window between left and right will satisfy the constraint — I'll expand right and shrink left to maintain this.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use prefix sums when you need to answer multiple range sum queries or when you're looking for a subarray with a specific sum. If the problem has multiple queries on the same array, prefix sum is almost always the right move.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Pre-compute prefix[i] = sum of elements from index 0 to i. Then any range sum from l to r is just prefix[r] - prefix[l-1]. This turns O(n) per query into O(1) per query after O(n) preprocessing. Combine with a hashmap to find subarrays with target sum in O(n).
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Off-by-one errors are everywhere here — be careful about 0-indexed vs 1-indexed prefix arrays. When using hashmap approach, initialize the map with {0: 1} before iterating to handle subarrays starting at index 0.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Mention: 'I'll precompute prefix sums so each range query is O(1) — the tradeoff is O(n) extra space which is worth it here.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use Kadane's for any 'maximum subarray sum' problem. It's also the base for 2D maximum subarray and circular subarray problems.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: At each index, you decide: extend the previous subarray or start fresh from this element. If current_sum + element < element, then the previous subarray is dragging you down — start fresh. Track the global maximum throughout. This is O(n) time, O(1) space.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: When all numbers are negative, many students return 0 instead of the least negative number. The question asks for max subarray sum, so if all are negative, the answer is the largest single element, not 0.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: State the decision at each step clearly: 'I'm choosing between extending the current window or starting a new one — whichever is larger becomes my running sum.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Any time the input is sorted and you need to find an element or its position, use binary search. Also trigger on 'find first occurrence', 'find last occurrence', or 'search in rotated sorted array'.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Maintain lo and hi pointers. Compute mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2 — never use (lo + hi) / 2 to avoid integer overflow. Eliminate half the search space each iteration based on how mid compares to target. This is O(log n).
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Writing mid = (lo + hi) / 2 causes integer overflow for large inputs — this is a real bug in interviews. Also, infinite loops happen when you don't properly update lo = mid + 1 or hi = mid - 1.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Mention the overflow fix proactively: 'I'm using lo + (hi - lo) / 2 to avoid integer overflow — it's a common gotcha.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use this when the problem asks to minimize the maximum or maximize the minimum of something. Phrases like 'what is the minimum number of X such that Y' often signal binary search on the answer space.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Instead of searching in an array, you binary search on the answer itself. Define a feasibility function that tells you 'is answer X possible?' — this function must be monotonic (if X works, X+1 also works, or vice versa). Binary search on the range of possible answers and call the feasibility function at each midpoint.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Students struggle to identify that the problem is binary-searchable at all. Practice recognizing the monotonic property. Also, getting the feasibility function wrong is the most common implementation bug.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Say: 'I notice the answer space is monotonic — I can binary search on the answer and check feasibility at each midpoint.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use merge sort when you need a stable O(n log n) sort, especially for linked lists. Also the base algorithm for counting inversions in an array.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Divide the array in half recursively until single elements, then merge sorted halves. Merging two sorted arrays is O(n) — you maintain two pointers and pick the smaller element each time. Total complexity is O(n log n) time, O(n) space for the auxiliary array.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Most students know merge sort conceptually but fumble the merge step in implementation. Practice writing the merge function separately — it's the heart of the algorithm and where bugs hide.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: For counting inversions, mention: 'When I merge and pick from right half first, all remaining elements in the left half form inversions — I count them there.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use quicksort when average-case O(n log n) with O(1) extra space is needed. Also important for the Kth largest element problem using QuickSelect.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Choose a pivot, partition the array so elements smaller than pivot are on the left and larger on the right, then recursively sort both halves. The partition step is O(n). Average case is O(n log n), worst case is O(n²) on already-sorted arrays — randomized pivot selection prevents this.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Implementing the partition step incorrectly is the #1 bug — practice Lomuto and Hoare partition schemes separately. Always randomize the pivot in interviews to show you know about worst-case behavior.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: For Kth largest: 'I'll use QuickSelect — same partition logic as quicksort but I only recurse into the half containing the Kth position, giving O(n) average.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use a hashmap whenever you need to count occurrences, check if an element has been seen before, or group elements by some property. 'Find duplicates', 'two sum', 'group anagrams' are all hashmap problems.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: A hashmap gives O(1) average insert and lookup. Build a frequency map by iterating once, then use it to answer queries in O(1). For two sum: store each number in the map as you go and check if complement exists — this is O(n) instead of O(n²).
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Using a sorted array + binary search instead of a hashmap when order doesn't matter — this is O(n log n) vs O(n). Also, not accounting for the same element being used twice in two-sum variants.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Always state the space tradeoff: 'I'm using O(n) extra space for the hashmap but this gets me from O(n²) to O(n) time — that's a good tradeoff here.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use a monotonic stack when you need the 'next greater element', 'next smaller element', 'previous greater element', or for problems involving spans and histograms. These are extremely common in interviews.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Maintain a stack where elements are always in increasing (or decreasing) order. When a new element breaks the monotonic property, pop elements from the stack — those popped elements have found their 'next greater/smaller'. Each element is pushed and popped at most once, giving O(n) total.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Students try to solve these problems with nested loops at O(n²) when the monotonic stack gives O(n). Also confusing when to use increasing vs decreasing stack — next greater needs decreasing stack, next smaller needs increasing.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Walk through a small example on the whiteboard first to convince yourself which direction the stack is monotonic before coding.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: This is a pure design/implementation problem that tests your understanding of stack and queue properties. It comes up often in interviews as a warm-up or in system design discussions.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Use two stacks — inbox and outbox. Push to inbox. For pop/peek, if outbox is empty, move all elements from inbox to outbox (this reverses the order, making it FIFO). If outbox isn't empty, just pop from it. Amortized O(1) per operation.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Students move elements back and forth between stacks on every operation — this is O(n) per operation. The key insight is you only transfer from inbox to outbox when outbox is completely empty.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Explain amortized analysis clearly: 'Each element is pushed and transferred at most once, so across n operations the total work is O(n) — O(1) amortized.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use Floyd's algorithm whenever you need to detect a cycle in a linked list, find the cycle start, or find duplicate numbers using the list-as-graph analogy.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Slow pointer moves one step, fast pointer moves two steps. If there's a cycle, they will definitely meet inside the cycle. To find the cycle start: after meeting, reset slow to head and advance both one step at a time — they meet at the cycle start. This is O(n) time, O(1) space.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Most students know the detection part but blank on finding the cycle start. Memorize the two-phase approach: phase 1 is meeting inside the cycle, phase 2 is finding the entry point with a fresh pointer from head.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: If asked why they meet at the cycle start, you don't need to prove it in an interview — just say 'there's a mathematical proof involving the cycle length' and move on to coding.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: List reversal is needed for palindrome checking, k-group reversals, and is a fundamental building block. If you can't reverse a linked list cleanly, you will struggle with harder list problems.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Three pointers: prev (null initially), current, and next. In each iteration, save next = current.next, point current.next = prev, advance prev = current, advance current = next. After the loop, prev is the new head. This is O(n) time, O(1) space.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Losing track of the next node before overwriting the pointer — always save next before you rewire. Students also forget that after reversal, the new head is prev, not current (current is null at the end).
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Draw three nodes on paper before coding — this problem is trivial to implement once you visualize it, but it's easy to fumble if you code without thinking.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use BFS/level order whenever the problem involves levels, depth, or layer-by-layer processing. 'Right side view', 'level averages', 'zig-zag traversal' are all BFS problems.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Use a queue. Enqueue the root. While the queue isn't empty, process all nodes at the current level by noting the queue size at the start of each level iteration. Enqueue children of each processed node. The queue size at the start of each level tells you exactly how many nodes belong to that level.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Not capturing the queue size at the start of the level loop — if you check queue.size() dynamically in the loop condition, you'll process nodes from the next level too.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Show the two-loop pattern: outer loop for levels, inner loop using a fixed level size — this cleanly separates levels without any extra data structure.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Inorder on BST gives sorted output. Preorder is used to serialize/copy a tree. Postorder is used when you need child information before processing the parent — like calculating subtree sizes or heights.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Inorder: left, root, right. Preorder: root, left, right. Postorder: left, right, root. For BST problems, inorder is almost always involved. For path problems, preorder (root-first) helps track paths from root to leaf. Each traversal is O(n) — you visit every node once.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Confusing which traversal to use. Remember: BST validation or sorted output = inorder. Tree construction or serialization = preorder. Bottom-up calculations (height, diameter) = postorder.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: State your traversal choice with reasoning: 'Since this is a BST and I need sorted order, I'll use inorder traversal which naturally gives me elements in sorted sequence.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: LCA appears in its pure form and as a building block for 'distance between nodes' and 'path between nodes' problems. Any time you need to find where two nodes diverge in a tree, think LCA.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: For a general binary tree: recurse. If current node is null or equals either target, return current node. Recurse left and right. If both return non-null, current node is the LCA. If only one side returns non-null, propagate that up. For BST: the first node where the values split between left and right subtrees is the LCA.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Trying to apply the BST method on a general binary tree — you can only use value comparisons on BSTs, not general trees. Also, not handling the case where one target is an ancestor of the other.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Clarify upfront: 'Is this a BST or a general binary tree?' — the approach differs significantly and asking this shows you know both.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use BFS for shortest path in unweighted graphs. 'Minimum steps', 'minimum moves', 'minimum number of operations' in a graph context all point to BFS. Dijkstra is for weighted graphs — BFS is simpler when all edges have equal weight.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: BFS explores nodes level by level — all nodes at distance 1 before distance 2, etc. The first time you reach the destination, that's guaranteed to be the shortest path. Use a visited set to avoid reprocessing nodes. BFS on a graph with V vertices and E edges is O(V + E).
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Not marking nodes as visited when you enqueue them (mark on enqueue, not on dequeue) — this causes the same node to be enqueued multiple times and can cause infinite loops or wrong answers.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Distinguish from Dijkstra explicitly: 'Since all edges have equal weight here, BFS gives shortest path in O(V+E) — no need for a priority queue.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use DFS to find connected components, detect cycles, topological sort, or explore all paths. 'Number of islands', 'provinces', 'friend groups' are all connected component problems.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Iterate over all nodes. For each unvisited node, run DFS — mark all reachable nodes as visited. Count how many times you start a fresh DFS — that's the number of connected components. For grids, DFS in 4 directions (up, down, left, right) works the same way.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: For grid problems, not handling boundaries (going out of bounds) is a very common bug. Also forgetting to mark a cell as visited before recursing, causing stack overflow from infinite recursion.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: For 'number of islands', walk through a 3x3 example with the interviewer before coding — it demonstrates clarity and catches edge cases early.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use topological sort for any problem involving dependencies or ordering with constraints. 'Course schedule', 'task ordering', 'build systems' are all topological sort problems. Works only on Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs).
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Calculate in-degree (number of incoming edges) for all nodes. Add all zero in-degree nodes to a queue. Process each: add to result, reduce in-degree of neighbors, enqueue any that reach zero. If result length equals total nodes, you have a valid topological order. If not, there's a cycle.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Students try to use DFS-based topological sort but struggle to implement cycle detection alongside it. Kahn's BFS approach detects cycles naturally — if the result doesn't include all nodes, there's a cycle.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: State the cycle detection bonus: 'With Kahn's algorithm, cycle detection is free — if I can't process all nodes, a cycle exists, which also tells me if the schedule is impossible.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use Dijkstra for single-source shortest path in graphs with non-negative edge weights. 'Minimum cost path', 'minimum time to reach', 'cheapest flight' problems all use Dijkstra.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Use a min-heap (priority queue). Start with source at distance 0. Always process the unvisited node with minimum current distance. For each neighbor, if new distance is shorter, update and push to heap. With a binary heap, complexity is O((V + E) log V).
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Dijkstra does NOT work with negative edge weights — use Bellman-Ford for that. Also, not skipping already-processed nodes when popping from the heap leads to redundant work (not incorrect, just slow).
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Mention the constraint clearly: 'Dijkstra requires non-negative edge weights — I'm confirming that here before applying it.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use DSU when you need to dynamically merge groups and check if two elements belong to the same group. 'Number of connected components', 'detecting cycles in undirected graph', and Kruskal's MST algorithm all use DSU.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Two operations: find (with path compression — point every node directly to root) and union (by rank — attach smaller tree under larger). With both optimizations, operations are nearly O(1) amortized — officially O(α(n)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Implementing DSU without path compression and union by rank is O(n) per operation in the worst case. Always implement both optimizations — they're simple and critical for performance.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Implement path compression iteratively in the find function — it's two lines of code and shows you know the optimization.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use 1D DP when the current state depends on one or two previous states. Climbing stairs, fibonacci, min cost climbing stairs, and house robber are all 1D DP problems.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Define dp[i] as the answer for problem of size i. Write the recurrence — how dp[i] depends on previous states. Initialize base cases (dp[0], dp[1]). Fill the table iteratively. Most 1D DP problems only need the last two values, so you can optimize to O(1) space.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Starting with memoization (top-down) is fine, but students often can't convert it to bottom-up tabulation, which interviewers prefer. Practice writing both forms — start recursive, then convert to iterative.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Always define dp[i] in words before writing any code: 'dp[i] represents the minimum cost to reach step i' — this forces you to think clearly and impresses interviewers.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use 0/1 knapsack when each item can be used at most once and you need to maximize value within a weight/capacity constraint. 'Subset sum', 'partition equal subset sum', 'target sum' are knapsack variants.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: dp[i][w] = max value using first i items with capacity w. For each item, you either include it (if it fits) or exclude it. Recurrence: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-weight[i]] + value[i]). Space can be optimized to O(W) by using a 1D array and iterating weight from right to left.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: When optimizing to 1D, iterating weight left to right causes an item to be counted multiple times (that's unbounded knapsack, not 0/1). Always iterate from capacity down to weight[i] for 0/1 knapsack.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Draw the 2D dp table for a 3-item example before coding — it takes 2 minutes and completely eliminates off-by-one errors in your implementation.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: LCS is the base for edit distance, longest palindromic subsequence, and shortest common supersequence. Any problem involving matching characters between two strings likely builds on LCS.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: dp[i][j] = LCS length of first i characters of s1 and first j characters of s2. If s1[i] == s2[j], dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1. Otherwise, dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Time is O(m*n), space is O(m*n) but can be optimized to O(min(m,n)).
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Confusing LCS (subsequence — doesn't need to be contiguous) with LCS (substring — must be contiguous). Longest Common Substring uses a different recurrence where you reset to 0 when characters don't match.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Clarify the difference immediately: 'Just to confirm — we want subsequence (non-contiguous) not substring (contiguous), right? The approach differs significantly.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: LIS appears directly and as a component in harder problems like 'Russian doll envelopes' and 'box stacking'. Recognize it when you need the longest sequence of elements in strictly increasing order.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: O(n²) DP: dp[i] = length of LIS ending at index i. For each i, check all j < i where arr[j] < arr[i] and take max. O(n log n) approach uses patience sorting with binary search — maintain a 'tails' array where tails[i] is the smallest tail of all increasing subsequences of length i+1.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Most students only know the O(n²) approach. The O(n log n) approach using binary search on the tails array is asked in senior interviews — practice it separately. The tails array doesn't store the actual LIS, just its length.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Start with O(n²) and mention you know an O(n log n) approach — if they ask for it, you can show it. Starting with the harder approach without being asked risks getting confused.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use matrix DP for any grid-based optimization problem: 'minimum path sum', 'unique paths', 'maximal square', 'dungeon game'. The grid structure naturally maps to a 2D DP table.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: dp[i][j] represents the answer at cell (i, j). Build it from top-left to bottom-right. The value at each cell depends on the cells above and to the left (or in some problems, below and to the right). Base cases are the first row and first column. Many grid DPs can be done in-place by modifying the input grid.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Not initializing the first row and column correctly as base cases. Also, for problems like 'unique paths with obstacles', forgetting to break the first row/column initialization when an obstacle is encountered.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: For in-place DP on grids: always confirm the interviewer is okay with modifying the input array — if not, say you'll use an extra O(m*n) table.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use backtracking for problems requiring you to generate all subsets, permutations, combinations, or to find all solutions to a constraint satisfaction problem. 'Generate all subsets', 'combination sum', 'N-queens' are canonical backtracking problems.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: At each step, make a choice, recurse, then undo the choice (backtrack). The recursion tree has all possibilities at each level. For subsets: at each element, decide include or exclude. For combinations: iterate from current index to avoid duplicates. Pruning the recursion tree based on constraints is what makes backtracking efficient.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Forgetting to remove the last element from the current path before backtracking — this corrupts the state for sibling branches. Always add to path before recursing and remove after recursing.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Draw the recursion tree for a 3-element example before coding — backtracking bugs almost always come from not thinking through the tree structure.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use a heap when you need repeated access to the minimum or maximum element, or to maintain the K largest/smallest elements. 'Kth largest element', 'merge K sorted arrays', 'top K frequent elements' all use heaps.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: A min-heap gives you O(1) access to minimum and O(log n) insert/delete. For 'K largest elements', use a min-heap of size K — when a new element is larger than the min (heap top), replace the top. This is O(n log K) total. Java has PriorityQueue (min by default), Python has heapq (min by default — negate values for max).
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Using a max-heap for K largest elements — you end up with O(n log n) because you store everything. A min-heap of size K is the efficient approach because you eject small elements early.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: In Python, simulate max-heap by pushing negative values — mention this explicitly: 'Python's heapq is a min-heap, so I'll negate values to simulate max-heap behavior.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use a trie for prefix-based search, autocomplete, word dictionaries, and problems involving common prefixes. If you're doing multiple prefix lookups on the same set of strings, a trie is far more efficient than repeated string matching.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Each node represents a character and has up to 26 children (for lowercase English). Insert by creating nodes for each character. Search checks if all characters exist in sequence. Mark end nodes with a boolean flag. Prefix search is O(L) where L is the prefix length — much better than O(L * N) for N strings.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Creating a trie with a 26-element array for children when most are null is memory-heavy for sparse tries — use a hashmap for children instead when the character set is large or sparse.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: If implementing from scratch, start with the TrieNode class, then write insert, then search, then startsWith — in exactly that order.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Whenever you need to check if strings are anagrams, find anagram groups, or find all anagrams in a string, this pattern applies. These appear in almost every company's interview.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Two strings are anagrams if they have identical character frequencies. Use a frequency array of size 26 for lowercase English — it's faster than sorting and cheaper than a hashmap. For 'find all anagrams in string S', use a sliding window of the pattern's length and maintain a running frequency count.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Sorting both strings to check anagrams is O(n log n) — the frequency array approach is O(n). Interviewers notice this. Also, forgetting to handle case sensitivity or spaces when the problem involves arbitrary strings.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Use an array of 26 integers instead of a hashmap — it's faster and simpler: int[] freq = new int[26]; then freq[c - 'a']++.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use KMP when you need O(n + m) string matching — naive is O(n*m). 'Find all occurrences of pattern in text', 'shortest repeating unit in string' use KMP. It's less commonly asked but impressive when you know it.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Precompute the LPS (Longest Proper Prefix which is also Suffix) array for the pattern. This tells you how far to jump back when a mismatch occurs instead of starting over. The LPS array is O(m) to compute. Matching is O(n). Total O(n + m).
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Most students either don't know KMP or can't implement the LPS array correctly under pressure. If you're unsure, it's better to code the naive O(n*m) approach correctly than to fumble through KMP incorrectly.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Mention KMP exists and that you know the concept, then implement it if the interviewer seems interested. Don't start with KMP if you haven't practiced it in the last week.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Tower of Hanoi isn't usually asked directly, but the pattern — solving n by solving n-1 twice — appears in merge sort, tree problems, and divide-and-conquer. Understanding it builds your recursion intuition.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: To move n disks from A to C via B: move n-1 disks from A to B (using C as helper), move disk n from A to C, move n-1 disks from B to C (using A as helper). The recurrence is T(n) = 2*T(n-1) + 1, which solves to O(2^n). This is unavoidable — the problem has exponential complexity.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Students try to optimize Tower of Hanoi — it can't be done below O(2^n) moves. If you're getting a non-exponential answer, your recursion is wrong.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Use Tower of Hanoi thinking for any problem where you need to 'get n-1 things out of the way' before solving for the nth — it's a reusable problem-solving pattern.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use segment trees when you have range queries (sum, min, max) combined with point or range updates, and need both to be O(log n). If the problem has many queries and updates on an array, segment tree beats prefix sum.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Build a complete binary tree where each leaf is an array element and each internal node stores the aggregate of its range. Build in O(n). Query any range in O(log n) by combining relevant nodes. Point update in O(log n) by updating all ancestors of the changed leaf.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Segment trees are overkill for problems with only queries (use prefix sum) or only updates (use direct access). Reach for them specifically when you have a mix of range queries AND updates.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: In product company interviews, if you mention segment trees, be ready to implement them — don't drop the name without knowing the code. Practice a clean implementation once.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use greedy for interval scheduling, meeting rooms, and activity selection. Whenever you need to maximize the number of non-overlapping intervals, always sort by end time and greedily pick.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Sort activities by end time. Always pick the activity that ends earliest and doesn't conflict with the last picked activity. This greedy choice is provably optimal — picking the earliest-ending activity always leaves maximum room for future activities. O(n log n) for sort, O(n) for selection.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Sorting by start time instead of end time is the classic wrong greedy. Sorting by duration is also wrong. End time is always the correct sort key for activity selection and most interval problems.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Justify your greedy choice to the interviewer: 'Picking the activity ending soonest maximizes future flexibility — this greedy choice is locally and globally optimal.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use greedy for jump game variants where you need to know if you can reach the end or the minimum jumps needed. These appear frequently in product company interviews.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Track the maximum index reachable at each position. At index i, the farthest you can reach is i + nums[i]. Update max_reach at each step. If at any point current index > max_reach, you're stuck. For minimum jumps: track current range boundary and count jumps when you exceed the boundary.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Trying to solve jump game with DP is O(n²) — the greedy approach is O(n). Most students default to DP because they see an optimization problem, but greedy is simpler and more efficient here.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: For minimum jumps, explain the two-pointer approach: 'I maintain current range and next range — when I exhaust current range I must jump, incrementing my count.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: XOR is your tool when you see 'find the single element', 'find two missing numbers', or any problem where pairing elements is key. XOR of a number with itself is 0, XOR with 0 is itself.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: XOR all elements — paired elements cancel out, leaving the single element. For finding two non-repeating elements: XOR gives combined XOR of both. Find a bit that's set (they differ in this bit). Split all elements into two groups based on this bit and XOR each group — you get both numbers.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Students reach for sorting or hashmaps for 'find single element' problems — XOR gives O(n) time and O(1) space, which is strictly better. Practice recognizing when XOR applies.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Mention the space advantage: 'XOR gives me O(1) space compared to hashmap's O(n) — for large arrays this is significant.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Beyond linked lists, Floyd's algorithm finds cycles in any functional graph — sequences defined by f(x) = next state. 'Find the duplicate number' (using array as implicit linked list) uses this exact technique.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Treat the array as a linked list where arr[i] points to arr[arr[i]]. A duplicate creates a cycle. Apply Floyd's to find the cycle, then the two-pointer reset technique to find the entry point — which is the duplicate value. O(n) time, O(1) space.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Students apply binary search or sorting for 'find duplicate in array without modifying it' — Floyd's gives a cleaner O(n)/O(1) solution. The key insight is modeling the array as a graph.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: For LeetCode 287, explicitly say: 'I'm treating the array as an implicit linked list where arr[i] is the next pointer — the duplicate creates a cycle entry point.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use a min-heap for merging K sorted arrays, K sorted linked lists, or finding the Kth smallest element across K sorted arrays. This is a fundamental interview pattern.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Push the first element of each of the K arrays into a min-heap along with the array index and element index. Pop the minimum, add to result, push the next element from the same array. Continue until all arrays are exhausted. Time: O(N log K) where N is total elements, space: O(K) for the heap.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Merging arrays two at a time (K-way merge in pairs) is O(N log K) but with a larger constant. The single heap approach processes each element exactly once with O(log K) per extraction.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Be specific about complexity: 'Each of the N total elements is pushed and popped once from a heap of size K — that's O(N log K) total, much better than O(NK) brute force.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use interval merging for 'merge all overlapping intervals', 'insert an interval', or any problem involving consolidating ranges. These appear in calendar and scheduling problems.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Sort intervals by start time. Maintain a result list. For each interval, if it overlaps with the last interval in result (current start <= last end), merge by updating the last interval's end to max(last end, current end). Otherwise, add current interval as new entry. O(n log n) for sort.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Checking overlap as 'start <= end' instead of 'start <= last.end' — the condition should use the last interval in the result, not some fixed interval. Also, sorting by end time instead of start time breaks the algorithm.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Walk through a 3-interval example with an overlap and without — shows you understand both branches of the algorithm before coding.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use a stack for any bracket matching, expression validation, or 'balanced parentheses' problem. This is a classic warm-up in interviews and appears as a subproblem in harder questions.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Push opening brackets onto the stack. When you see a closing bracket, check if the stack top is the matching opener — if yes, pop; if no or stack is empty, the string is invalid. At the end, the string is valid only if the stack is empty. O(n) time, O(n) space.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Not checking if the stack is empty before popping when you see a closing bracket — this throws an exception. Always check isEmpty() first. Also forgetting to check if the stack is empty at the end.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: This is often asked as a warm-up to gauge your comfort with stacks — solve it confidently and cleanly, it sets the tone for the rest of the interview.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use deque (double-ended queue) for sliding window maximum/minimum problems. The deque maintains a monotonic structure so the front always holds the index of the max/min for the current window.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: For sliding window maximum: maintain a deque of indices in decreasing order of their values. For each new element, remove from back all indices with smaller values. Remove from front any index that's outside the current window. Front of deque is always the max. O(n) total — each element is added and removed at most once.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Using a regular queue or array and recomputing the max for each window is O(n*k). The deque approach is O(n) because each index enters and exits the deque exactly once.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: The deque stores indices, not values — this lets you check if elements are still within the window. Emphasize this when explaining your approach.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: BST validation appears directly and as a conceptual check in interviews. Interviewers use it to test if you understand BST invariants beyond just 'left < root < right'.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Pass down min and max bounds through recursion. Root has bounds (-inf, +inf). Left child has bounds (min, root.val). Right child has bounds (root.val, max). A node is invalid if its value is outside its bounds. The common mistake of just comparing to parent is insufficient because ancestors impose tighter constraints.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Checking only that left.val < root.val < right.val is wrong — it doesn't account for the BST property across the entire path. A node in the right subtree must be greater than ALL its ancestors, not just its direct parent.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: This is a classic trap problem — about 40% of candidates give the wrong answer initially. Walk through a counterexample tree before coding to show you know the gotcha.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use Bellman-Ford for shortest paths with negative edge weights, or to detect negative cycles. If the problem says 'minimum cost with possible negative weights' or 'detect negative cycle', Bellman-Ford is your tool.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Relax all edges V-1 times. Each relaxation pass guarantees shortest paths of at most i edges after i passes. If a Vth pass still improves distances, a negative cycle exists. O(V*E) time — much slower than Dijkstra's O((V+E) log V), but handles negative weights.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Using Dijkstra on a graph with negative weights — it will give wrong answers. This is a correctness issue, not a performance issue. Always check for negative weights and switch to Bellman-Ford.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Problems about 'cheapest flight within k stops' are Bellman-Ford variants — run exactly k passes of relaxation and only relax with prices from the previous iteration (use a copy).

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use MST algorithms when you need to connect all nodes with minimum total edge weight. 'Minimum cost to connect all cities', 'network design' problems are MST problems.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Sort all edges by weight. Use DSU to greedily add edges that don't create a cycle. After processing all edges, you'll have an MST with V-1 edges connecting V nodes. O(E log E) for sort, nearly O(E) for DSU operations. Alternative: Prim's algorithm using a priority queue is better for dense graphs.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Students confuse MST (connects all nodes with min total weight) with shortest path (min distance between two specific nodes) — these are different problems with different algorithms.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Kruskal's and Prim's give the same MST result — mention you know both and pick Kruskal's (simpler to implement) unless the graph is dense, where Prim's with adjacency matrix is O(V²).

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Coin change (minimum coins) is unbounded knapsack. Coin change 2 (number of ways) is the ways variant. These patterns appear in 'minimum operations', 'minimum cost', and 'count ways' problems.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: For minimum coins: dp[amount] = min coins to make that amount. Start dp[0] = 0, rest infinity. For each coin, for each amount from coin to target, dp[a] = min(dp[a], dp[a-coin] + 1). For counting ways: dp[amount] counts distinct combinations — iterate coins in outer loop to avoid counting permutations.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Coin change 2 requires iterating coins in the outer loop and amounts in the inner loop. Reversing this counts permutations (different order = different way) instead of combinations.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: State clearly which variant you're solving: 'I'm solving for minimum coins, not number of ways — the DP direction changes based on which variant it is.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use DP for 'can this string be segmented into dictionary words'. This pattern extends to 'word break II' (enumerate all segmentations) and 'concatenated words'.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: dp[i] = true if s[0..i-1] can be segmented into dictionary words. For each position i, check all j < i where dp[j] is true and s[j..i-1] is in the dictionary. Use a hashset for O(1) dictionary lookup. Overall O(n² * L) where L is max word length.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Using recursion without memoization is exponential — you recompute the same sub-strings many times. Always memoize or use bottom-up DP.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Optimize the inner loop by only checking substrings up to the maximum word length in the dictionary — this prunes unnecessary iterations.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Bipartite check is asked directly and is a component of matching algorithms. A graph is bipartite if it contains no odd-length cycles — use BFS/DFS 2-coloring to verify.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Try to 2-color the graph using BFS or DFS. Color the start node with color 0, color all neighbors with color 1, their neighbors with 0, etc. If you ever need to color a node with a color it already has (different from what you're assigning), the graph is not bipartite.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Checking only one connected component and declaring the whole graph bipartite — iterate over all nodes and start BFS/DFS for each unvisited node. A disconnected graph's bipartiteness depends on all components.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: This is often framed as 'can you divide students into two groups where no two in the same group are rivals?' — recognize it as a graph coloring problem.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use rolling hash (Rabin-Karp) for string pattern matching, finding duplicate substrings, or any problem where you need to hash many substrings efficiently.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Compute a polynomial hash for a window of characters. Slide the window: remove the leftmost character's contribution and add the new rightmost character's. This gives O(1) hash updates after O(n) preprocessing. Use mod to keep values manageable and double-hash to reduce collision probability.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Using a single hash and assuming no collisions — always verify a hash match with actual string comparison to avoid false positives. Rolling hash tells you 'probably equal', string comparison confirms it.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Rabin-Karp is rarely asked in Indian product company interviews — mention it if you know it, but don't let not knowing it block you. KMP and brute force are more commonly tested.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Diameter of tree (longest path between any two nodes) comes up directly and in variants like 'diameter of N-ary tree'. The same pattern also solves 'longest path in binary tree' and 'maximum path sum'.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: For each node, the longest path through it is left_height + right_height. Run DFS and at each node, update a global maximum with the sum of left and right subtree heights. Return max(left, right) + 1 as the height for the parent. O(n) — single DFS pass.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Trying to find diameter with two separate DFS calls (find farthest node from root, then farthest from that) — this works for unweighted trees but requires knowing the proof. The single-DFS postorder approach is simpler and generalizes better.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: The key insight is: the diameter might not pass through the root. Your DFS must check every node as a potential midpoint — the global variable updated at each node handles this.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Articulation points (bridges) appear in network reliability problems. 'Find critical connections in a network' is LeetCode's version. These are rarely asked in standard product company interviews but appear in top-tier companies.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Use DFS with discovery time and low values. Low[v] = minimum discovery time reachable from v's subtree (including back edges). A node u is an articulation point if it has a child v where low[v] >= disc[u]. The root is an articulation point if it has more than one DFS child.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Confusing articulation points (removing a node disconnects graph) with bridges (removing an edge disconnects graph) — they use similar algorithms but the condition differs slightly.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: This is genuinely hard to implement from scratch in an interview. If asked, explain the approach clearly first — partial credit for explaining the algorithm is better than fumbling through incorrect code.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Palindrome problems are everywhere — checking, finding longest palindromic substring, finding longest palindromic subsequence. They come in both string and linked list forms.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: For checking: two pointers from both ends meeting in the middle — O(n). For longest palindromic substring: expand around center for each of the 2n-1 possible centers (n single characters and n-1 gaps between). O(n²) time, O(1) space. Manacher's algorithm does it in O(n) but is rarely needed in interviews.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Forgetting that a palindrome can have an even length (like 'abba') — you must check both odd and even center cases. Students who only check odd centers miss half the possibilities.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: For longest palindromic substring, two-pointer expand-around-center is the go-to — O(n²) time and O(1) space. If the interviewer pushes for O(n), mention Manacher's.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Edit distance (Levenshtein) is the base for spell checkers, DNA sequence alignment, and diff tools. It's asked directly and as a follow-up to LCS problems.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: dp[i][j] = minimum edit operations to convert s1[0..i-1] to s2[0..j-1]. Base cases: dp[i][0] = i, dp[0][j] = j. If characters match: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]. If not: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) for delete, insert, replace.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Getting the three operations mixed up: dp[i-1][j] is delete from s1, dp[i][j-1] is insert into s1 (delete from s2), dp[i-1][j-1] is replace. Draw the operations on a small example to keep them clear.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Relate to LCS: 'Edit distance is related to LCS — minimum edits = n + m - 2*LCS_length. Knowing this connection shows depth.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Binary search applies to any problem where you can eliminate half the search space based on local information. Finding peak elements, finding minimum in rotated sorted array, and aggressive cows all use this pattern.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: A peak exists where arr[mid] > arr[mid-1] and arr[mid] > arr[mid+1]. If arr[mid] < arr[mid+1], there's a peak on the right (right side is going up). If arr[mid] < arr[mid-1], there's a peak on the left. This guarantee exists because array boundaries count as -infinity.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Thinking binary search only works on fully sorted arrays — it works on any function where you can make a binary decision about which half contains the answer. Peak element is the classic example.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Justify why binary search is valid here: 'The slope tells me which direction has a peak — if I'm going uphill to the right, a peak must exist on that side.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: SCCs appear in problems involving condensation of directed graphs, finding groups where everyone can reach everyone. Rarely tested in standard Indian placement interviews but expected at FAANG level.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Two DFS passes: first DFS on original graph, push nodes to stack in finish order. Transpose the graph (reverse all edges). Second DFS from nodes in reverse finish order on transposed graph — each DFS tree in second pass is one SCC. O(V + E).
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Confusing Kosaraju's (two DFS on original + transposed) with Tarjan's (one DFS with low-link values) — both find SCCs but differently. Know at least one well rather than both poorly.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Tarjan's is often preferred since it's a single pass. If you know Tarjan's, use it — it demonstrates stronger algorithmic knowledge.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Array rotation and cyclic rotation appear in their own right and in rotated sorted array problems. If you're asked to rotate in-place, the reversal algorithm is the standard approach.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: To rotate array right by k: reverse entire array, reverse first k elements, reverse remaining n-k elements. This is O(n) time and O(1) space — no extra array needed. For rotating a matrix 90 degrees: transpose (swap arr[i][j] and arr[j][i]) then reverse each row.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Allocating an extra array of size n for rotation — the problem usually says in-place. The reversal trick achieves O(1) space. Also, forgetting to take k modulo n when k > n.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Practice the reversal trick until it's automatic — it's asked surprisingly often and fumbling an 'easy' problem on rotation leaves a bad impression.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Tree serialization is tested as a design problem at FAANG level. It requires understanding both DFS/BFS traversals and how to reconstruct the tree.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Preorder serialization: write 'root,left,right'. Use 'null' or '#' as sentinels for missing children. Deserialization: build a queue of tokens, recursively reconstruct — the first token is root, then recurse for left subtree (which consumes tokens), then right. BFS-based serialization is also valid.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Using inorder alone — you can't reconstruct a tree from just inorder traversal. Preorder + inorder works, but preorder with null markers is self-contained and simpler.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Ask: 'Should I optimize for space in the serialized string, or is simplicity fine?' — showing you consider tradeoffs impresses interviewers even if they say simplicity is fine.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Task scheduling with cooldown periods is a greedy + counting problem. 'Minimum time to execute all tasks with cooldown n' uses frequency-based greedy.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Count task frequencies. The most frequent task determines the structure. Place the most frequent task first, fill cooldown slots with other tasks or idle time. Total time = max(total tasks, (max_freq - 1) * (n + 1) + count of tasks with max frequency).
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Simulating the process step by step is too slow for large inputs. The formula-based approach comes from recognizing the structure: the most frequent tasks create 'frames' and everything else fills those frames.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Derive the formula during the interview by drawing the grid of task frames — 'I can visualize this as filling a grid with max_freq rows and (n+1) columns.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Two sum and its variants (three sum, four sum, two sum II with sorted array) are in almost every company's question bank. Master the pattern and its variations.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Two Sum: hashmap for O(n). Two Sum II (sorted): two pointers for O(n). Three Sum: fix one element, two-pointer on the rest — sort first, skip duplicates, O(n²). Four Sum: fix two, two-pointer on remaining — O(n³). The pattern scales by adding one nested loop per additional element.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: For Three Sum, students forget to skip duplicates for both the fixed element and the two pointers — this causes duplicate triplets in output. Add 'while arr[i] == arr[i-1]: skip' at each level.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: When you see K sum for K > 2, reduce it to K-1 sum recursively — this is a generalizable pattern you can explain to the interviewer.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: String-based calculator problems (evaluate +, -, *, / with or without parentheses) are tested at product companies. They test both stack understanding and careful implementation.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: For basic calculator without parentheses: maintain a stack of numbers and signs. Process number, then operator, then number. For parentheses: push current result and sign onto stack when you see '(', pop and combine when you see ')'. Multiplication/division have higher precedence — handle before adding to stack.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Not handling multi-digit numbers — you need to accumulate digits, not process each digit individually. Also, final number after the loop isn't added to the stack until a non-digit forces processing.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Test your solution with '3+2*2', '(3+2)*2', and '-1+2' — these three examples cover precedence, parentheses, and leading negatives.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use memoization when your recursive solution has overlapping subproblems — you compute the same state multiple times. If your recursion tree has duplicate nodes, add a cache.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Add a HashMap (or array) that maps state to result. Before computing, check the cache. After computing, store in cache before returning. This transforms exponential recursion into polynomial time. The cache key is the full state — everything that makes two recursive calls different.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Forgetting to include all relevant state in the cache key — if two situations are identical in the key but differ in some other way that affects the result, your cache gives wrong answers.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Start with a clean recursive solution, verify it works on small examples, then add memoization — this two-step approach produces cleaner code than trying to write DP bottom-up directly.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use 0/1 BFS when edges have only two possible weights: 0 and 1. Instead of Dijkstra (O((V+E) log V)), you can solve this in O(V+E) using a deque.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Use a deque instead of a regular queue. For a 0-weight edge, push the neighbor to the front. For a 1-weight edge, push to the back. This maintains the invariant that the front of the deque always has the minimum distance node, without needing a heap.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Using Dijkstra with a priority queue for 0/1 weighted graphs — it's correct but unnecessarily slow. Recognize the special structure and use the deque optimization.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: This is a niche optimization — mention it only if the interviewer specifically asks about optimizing Dijkstra or if the graph clearly has only 0/1 weights.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use DP for minimum cuts to make all partitions palindromes, or to enumerate all palindrome partitions. This combines palindrome checking with DP.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Precompute isPalin[i][j] for all substrings in O(n²). Then dp[i] = minimum cuts for s[0..i]. For each i, try all j <= i where s[j..i] is a palindrome: dp[i] = min(dp[j-1] + 1). Base case: if s[0..i] itself is a palindrome, dp[i] = 0. Total O(n²).
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Recomputing palindrome checks inside the DP — this makes it O(n³). Always precompute the palindrome table first as a separate step.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Explain the two-step approach clearly: 'I'll first build a palindrome lookup table in O(n²), then use it to fill the DP in O(n²) — total O(n²) with O(n²) space.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Use Dutch National Flag (3-way partition) for 'sort colors' (sort array with 3 distinct values) and as a subroutine for 3-way quicksort. Also useful for partitioning around a pivot with equal elements.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Maintain three pointers: low (all elements < pivot), mid (current), high (all elements > pivot). Elements between low and mid are equal to pivot. Scan with mid: if less than pivot, swap with low and advance both; if equal, advance mid only; if greater, swap with high and only decrease high.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: When swapping with high and decrementing high, don't advance mid — the newly swapped element at mid hasn't been examined yet. This is the most common off-by-one in this algorithm.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Invariant-based explanation: 'At all times, arr[0..low-1] < pivot, arr[low..mid-1] = pivot, arr[high+1..n-1] > pivot — mid explores the unknown middle.' Explaining invariants impresses interviewers.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: N-Queens is the prototypical constraint satisfaction problem. It directly tests backtracking and is asked at FAANG and top product companies. Also tests your ability to define conflict checks efficiently.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Place one queen per row. For each row, try all columns. A placement is valid if no previous queen is in the same column, same main diagonal, or same anti-diagonal. Track three sets: cols, diagonals (row-col), anti-diagonals (row+col). If valid, recurse to next row; if N queens placed, record solution.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Checking validity by scanning all previously placed queens is O(n) per check. Using the three sets (cols, diag, anti-diag) reduces it to O(1) per check.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: The diagonal trick (row-col is constant on a diagonal, row+col on anti-diagonal) is elegant — mention it explicitly as it shows you thought about the structure, not just the brute force.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Bit counting and bit manipulation problems test your knowledge of bitwise operators. 'Count set bits', 'power of two', 'single number' are standard bit manipulation questions.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Brian Kernighan's trick: n & (n-1) clears the rightmost set bit. Repeat until n = 0, counting iterations. For counting bits for all numbers 0 to n: dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1) — the bit count of i is one more than i/2 if i is odd. O(n) time, O(n) space.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Using a loop to check each of the 32 bits is O(32) per number — not wrong, but less elegant. Brian Kernighan's is O(set bits) which is often faster in practice.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Know these bit tricks by heart: n & (n-1) removes lowest set bit, n & (-n) isolates lowest set bit, n ^ n = 0, n ^ 0 = n. They appear constantly.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Find median from a data stream is a classic design problem using two heaps. It appears at FAANG level and tests your ability to maintain invariants across multiple operations.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Maintain a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half. The max-heap's top is the largest small element, min-heap's top is the smallest large element. Keep sizes balanced: difference at most 1. Median is max-heap's top (odd total) or average of both tops (even total). O(log n) per insert, O(1) for median.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Not maintaining the balance invariant after each insertion. The standard approach: always insert to max-heap, then push max-heap's top to min-heap, then balance sizes. Practice this exact sequence.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: This is a beautiful problem — spend 2 minutes explaining the invariant before coding. 'I'll maintain max-heap size >= min-heap size, with difference at most 1 — median comes from tops.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Minimum window substring is the prototypical variable sliding window problem. Any problem asking for the 'smallest window containing all of X' uses this exact pattern.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Use two frequency maps: required (from pattern) and window (current window). Track 'formed' count — how many distinct characters in window have required frequency. Expand right to include characters, shrink left when all characters are satisfied. O(|S| + |T|) time.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Not decrementing formed when shrinking makes a character's count drop below required. The formed variable must accurately reflect whether all requirements are currently satisfied.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: This is hard under pressure — practice it until the two-map + formed variable pattern is automatic. It appears in 5-10% of product company interviews.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Path sum variants (root to leaf, any path, path equals target) are extremely common. 'Binary tree maximum path sum' is a hard variant that frequently appears in FAANG interviews.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: For root-to-leaf: recursive DFS, carry running sum, check at leaves. For any-path max sum: at each node, the max gain from left subtree is max(0, left_gain) — ignore negative subtrees. Update global max with left_gain + right_gain + node.val. Return max(left, right) gain + node.val to parent.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: For maximum path sum, returning the full path through the node to the parent — you can only return one branch (left or right) up the tree since a path can't fork. The global variable captures the forked path.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Distinguish what you return vs what you record: 'I return max gain for parent to use (one branch), but I record the full path through this node in the global maximum.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Next permutation is asked directly and as an understanding check. It also underlies permutation enumeration. Knowing the O(n) algorithm in-place is expected.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Find the first decreasing element from the right (index i). Find the smallest element to the right of i that's larger than arr[i] (index j). Swap arr[i] and arr[j]. Reverse the subarray from i+1 to end. This gives the next lexicographically greater permutation in O(n) time.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Not reversing the suffix after the swap — just swapping arr[i] and arr[j] doesn't give the next permutation. The suffix must be reversed to get the smallest possible arrangement of those elements.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Trace through '132' → '213' step by step before coding: i=1 (3>2 breaks decreasing), j=2 (3 is smallest > arr[1]=3, wait — arr[j]=2... work through this carefully to get the steps right.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Word ladder and similar transformation problems are BFS problems in disguise. Each word is a node, edges exist between words that differ by one character. Minimum transformations = shortest path.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: BFS from start word. For each word in the queue, generate all possible one-character transformations and check if they're in the word list. If valid, add to queue and remove from word list (marking visited). First time you reach end word, that's the minimum distance. Use a set for O(1) word lookups.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Generating neighbors by comparing every word in the dictionary with current word — O(n * L) per step. Generating all L*26 transformations and checking against the set is O(L*26) per step — much faster when dictionary is large.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Bidirectional BFS (expand from both source and target simultaneously) cuts the search space significantly — mention this as an optimization if the interviewer asks.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: This interval DP pattern applies to any problem where the order of operations on intervals matters and removing one element affects others. It's a hard problem that tests whether you can think in intervals.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Instead of thinking about which balloon to burst first, think about which balloon to burst last in the interval [i, j]. dp[i][j] = max coins from bursting all balloons in (i, j) exclusive, with boundary balloons i and j remaining. This avoids the 'depends on what's been removed' complication.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Thinking forward (which to burst first) instead of backward (which to burst last) — the forward direction has dependencies that make the DP state unclear. The 'last to burst' formulation makes the subproblems independent.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: This is a genuinely hard DP and it's okay to take time. Explain the key insight: 'I'll think about which balloon to burst last in each interval — this avoids tracking which balloons are gone.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Morris Traversal achieves O(1) space tree traversal (no stack, no recursion) by temporarily modifying the tree. It's asked at companies that want to see if you can do in-order traversal with O(1) extra space.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Find the in-order predecessor of each node. Create a temporary link from predecessor to current node. Traverse using these links, then restore the original structure when revisiting. The tree is restored exactly, so while it's modified temporarily, the final state is unchanged.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: This algorithm temporarily modifies the tree — in a concurrent system, this could cause bugs. In interviews, mention this limitation to show systems awareness.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: If asked about O(1) space tree traversal, describe Morris Traversal conceptually first. Implementing it from memory under pressure is risky — the temporary link creation and restoration has tricky details.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Fractional knapsack (where you can take fractions of items) has a greedy solution. This is different from 0/1 knapsack which needs DP. Distinguish them immediately in interviews.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Calculate value/weight ratio for each item. Sort by ratio descending. Take items greedily — if full item fits, take it; otherwise take fraction. O(n log n) for sort. Greedy works here because taking more of a high-ratio item is always better than taking less.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Applying DP to fractional knapsack — it works but O(n*W) when O(n log n) greedy exists. Worse, applying greedy to 0/1 knapsack — it doesn't always work for the discrete case. Know which version needs which approach.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Always clarify: 'Is this fractional (can take parts) or 0/1 (whole items only)?' — this shows you know the fundamental difference.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Trapping rain water is a classic and appears regularly at Amazon, Google, and Indian product companies. It has O(n²) brute force, O(n) with prefix arrays, and O(n) O(1) space with two pointers.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Water trapped at position i = min(max_left[i], max_right[i]) - height[i]. Precompute left max and right max arrays in two passes: O(n) time, O(n) space. Two-pointer optimization: whichever side has smaller current max processes first — water at that position is min(left_max, right_max) - height, and left_max or right_max is the current side's max.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: The two-pointer O(1) space approach confuses students because it's not obvious why processing the smaller side first is correct. Practice the proof: the water level is limited by the minimum of left and right max, so the smaller max side fully determines the water at its pointer.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Solve it with prefix arrays first, then optimize to two pointers when asked — showing the optimization path demonstrates problem-solving thinking.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Alien dictionary is topological sort on characters. Whenever you need to infer an ordering from pairwise comparisons, model as a directed graph and topological sort.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Compare adjacent words in the list — find the first character that differs. That difference gives a directed edge (earlier word's char comes before later word's char). Build the graph, then topological sort (Kahn's). If the sorted result has fewer characters than the unique character count, a cycle exists (invalid).
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Not handling the case where a longer word appears before its prefix — e.g., 'abc' before 'ab' is invalid regardless of alphabet order. Detect this by checking if words[i] starts with words[i+1] and is longer.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: This is a two-step problem: graph construction (from word comparisons) and then topological sort. Clearly separate these steps in your explanation before coding.

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Stock problems form a progression: single transaction (greedy), unlimited transactions (greedy), at most 2 transactions (DP), at most k transactions (DP), with cooldown (DP), with fee (DP). Know them all.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: For at most k transactions: dp[k][i] = max profit using at most k transactions up to day i. For unlimited: add all positive differences greedily. For single transaction: track min price seen so far, profit = price - min. For with cooldown: states are held, sold, rest — transitions between states.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Treating stock problems as one problem with one solution. Each variant has a meaningfully different solution. In interviews, clarify which variant before coding.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: If asked the single transaction version but you know the unlimited version, solve the asked version first, then mention: 'I also know the unlimited transactions and k-transactions variants if you want to extend this.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Generate all valid parentheses combinations is a controlled backtracking problem. It tests whether you can apply constraints during generation rather than generating all and filtering.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Recursively build strings. At each step, add '(' if open count < n. Add ')' if close count < open count. Recurse with incremented counts. Base case: string length = 2n. The count-based constraint ensures you never have more closes than opens, guaranteeing validity without post-filtering.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Generating all 2^(2n) strings and filtering valid ones — this is exponentially slower than the constrained generation which produces only Catalan(n) strings, all valid.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: Explain why constraints are applied during generation: 'By tracking open and close counts, I only generate valid strings — no post-filtering needed. This produces exactly the Catalan number count of results.'

dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Meeting rooms problems test interval knowledge. 'Minimum rooms needed' = maximum overlapping intervals at any time = heap or sweep line problem.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: For minimum rooms: sort by start time. Use a min-heap of end times. For each meeting, if start time >= heap's minimum (earliest ending meeting), that meeting ended — pop it. Push current meeting's end time. Heap size at the end = minimum rooms. O(n log n).
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Solving this by brute force (checking every pair of meetings) is O(n²). The heap approach processes each meeting once. Also, 'can all meetings happen' (no overlap) is simpler — just sort by start and check each consecutive pair.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: The heap at any point holds all meetings currently in progress — its size is the current room usage. Maximum heap size over all meetings is your answer.

dsa_concepts.topic: DP — Minimum Path Sum in Triangle
dsa_concepts.when_to_use: Triangle DP and variable-width row problems are about recognizing the transition — from row i, you can reach adjacent elements in row i+1. This is similar to grid DP but with a triangle shape.
dsa_concepts.core_idea: Bottom-up: start from the last row and work up. For each cell, dp[i][j] = triangle[i][j] + min(dp[i+1][j], dp[i+1][j+1]). Optimize space by using a 1D array of size n+1 and updating in-place from left to right. The answer is dp[0][0] after processing all rows.
dsa_concepts.common_mistakes: Going top-down without memoization causes repeated subproblem computation. Bottom-up is cleaner here. Also, 1D space optimization requires careful index management — dry run once before coding.
dsa_concepts.interview_tip: This problem allows O(n) space optimization (one row array) — mentioning this proactively shows you think about space beyond just correctness.

company_patterns.rounds: Online assessment (2 DSA problems, 90 mins), then 4-5 interview rounds: 1 coding, 2 bar raiser rounds combining DSA + LLD, 1 behavioral focusing on leadership principles, sometimes a system design round for SDE-2 level hires.
company_patterns.focus_areas: Amazon is obsessed with Leadership Principles — every interview weaves them in. Technically they focus heavily on trees, graphs, DP, and system design. Expect OOP design questions and questions about scalability.
company_patterns.oa_pattern: Two medium-hard LeetCode questions in 90 minutes. They often use workstyle assessment questions alongside coding — don't rush these. Amazon OA is harder than most companies at this stage.
company_patterns.red_flags: Not using STAR format in behavioral questions gets you rejected even if coding is perfect. Not being able to articulate tradeoffs in system design is also a rejection signal.
company_patterns.prep_advice: Memorize at least 5 STAR stories mapped to Amazon's 16 Leadership Principles before the interview. For coding, focus on medium-hard array, tree, and graph problems on LeetCode. Practice explaining your thought process out loud — Amazon interviews heavily on communication.

company_patterns.rounds: Phone screen (1 coding problem, 45 mins), then 4-5 onsite rounds mixing coding and system design. Each round is a separate interviewer. Google does not have a fixed 'HR round' — every round is technical or googliness (culture fit).
company_patterns.focus_areas: Google cares most about problem-solving clarity, code quality, and the ability to handle follow-up questions. They want to see you optimize solutions on the fly. Coding speed matters less than clarity of thought.
company_patterns.oa_pattern: Google's OA for campus hiring has 2-3 problems with increasing difficulty. They sometimes include output-based questions where you predict the output of code — unusual but real.
company_patterns.red_flags: Writing code without thinking through the approach first, or being unable to come up with optimizations when the interviewer hints at them. Also, messy code with poor variable names is noticed.
company_patterns.prep_advice: LeetCode medium-hard problems with clean code are the standard. Practice thinking aloud and asking clarifying questions before coding. Study hash tables, trees, graphs, sorting, and complexity analysis deeply. NeetCode 150 is a well-organized prep list for Google.

company_patterns.rounds: OA with 2-3 coding problems, then 3-4 interview rounds alternating between coding and behavioral. Final round is with a senior hiring manager — more conversation than coding.
company_patterns.focus_areas: Microsoft tests fundamental DSA (arrays, strings, trees, linked lists) more than advanced graph theory or DP. They care a lot about code quality and writing clean, maintainable code rather than squeezing every nanosecond.
company_patterns.oa_pattern: Microsoft's OA is on Codility or their own platform. Problems are typically LeetCode medium difficulty. They have some system design questions in OA for experienced roles.
company_patterns.red_flags: Not asking clarifying questions before coding, producing buggy code without testing, and poor behavioral answers about teamwork and handling failure.
company_patterns.prep_advice: Focus on medium LeetCode problems and be able to explain your complexity clearly. Prepare 3-4 stories about collaboration and dealing with failure — Microsoft culture fit matters. Practice on Codility since their OA platform has slightly different behavior.

company_patterns.rounds: 2 technical phone screens followed by 4 onsite rounds: 2 coding, 1 system design, 1 behavioral (product sense). Behavioral round is structured — they use specific questions about your past work.
company_patterns.focus_areas: Meta heavily tests dynamic programming, graphs, and arrays. They want optimal solutions — if there's an O(n) solution and you give O(n log n), they'll push you to do better. System design is crucial for E4+ hires.
company_patterns.oa_pattern: Meta's OA has 2 coding problems with test cases that include edge cases. They also have a coding assessment where you complete a larger partial problem.
company_patterns.red_flags: Giving suboptimal solutions without attempting to optimize, poor time management in coding rounds, and giving vague answers in behavioral that don't use specific examples.
company_patterns.prep_advice: Practice hard DP and graph problems. Meta expects you to arrive at the optimal solution, not just a working one. Study system design for distributed systems — news feed, messaging, photo storage are classic Meta designs.

company_patterns.rounds: More conversational than other FAANGs. Phone screen focused on technical fundamentals, then 4-6 onsite rounds with different team members. Apple hiring is decentralized — each team hires differently.
company_patterns.focus_areas: Apple cares about fundamentals: OS concepts, memory management, data structures. For iOS roles, deep Swift and Objective-C knowledge. For backend, strong system design. Less LeetCode-grind focused than other FAANGs.
company_patterns.oa_pattern: Apple doesn't always have an OA for campus hiring in India — they sometimes go straight to phone screens. For iOS roles, there might be a take-home assignment.
company_patterns.red_flags: Weak knowledge of OS concepts (memory, processes, threads) for systems roles. Not knowing the Apple ecosystem well enough for product-facing roles.
company_patterns.prep_advice: Apple hires fewer freshers from India compared to other FAANGs. Focus on WWDC if applying for iOS roles.
